Erred JOKE

pages.raw_text:
The growth of the dry farming
movement is one of the wonders
of the present age. A term that
was provocative of incredulOus
smiles and deprecatory jests five
years ago, today Is recognized as
symbolicial of the higher agri-
culture toward which all nations
are striving. The first meeting at
which the Trans-Missouri Dry
Farming congress was formed, was
regarded as a joke, the fifth Dry
Farming Congress which will meet
in Spokane, holds the serious at
tention of nations.


Five years ago there were a
few farmers struggling against
adversely on the plains Of eastern
Colorado, in New Mexico, Utah
and elsewhere in the semi-arid
west. The settler who took up
land in non-irrigable sections of
the west were regarded as fool
hardy, or worse. Their efforts to
make the dry lands productive
were condemned as inviting moth
ing but failure.


SOME NOTABLE EXCEPTIONS
Many settlers who had gone into
the dry regions did fail and many
struggled along for years eking out
bare existence with the aid OF
few exceptionally propitious
years, but numbers succeeded
Scattered throughout the west in
Utah, Wyoming, Montana, Color
ado and in other states were more
or less isolated exception to the
expectations of the pessimists. In
some sections, notably in eastern
Washington and Oregon and Utah
there were notable exceptions, and
in these states dry land farming
was accepted as fact.


Five years ago the newspapers
of the country were loath to pub.
lish articles of the successes OF
dry farming. There was pre-.
valent idea throughout the east
that the greater part of the west
was useless for farming; that des'
ert land would remain barren and
desolate where the irrigating ditch
was unknown, and that so called
dry farming was ridiculous in con
ception and impracticable in ap
plication.


FAILURES CHARGED TO IGNoR


ANCE


The failures that have been re
corded among the settlers on the
dry lands and the prejudice in
the minds of eastern and western
skeptics against dry farming lat
a common origin-in ignorance Of
the methods necessary to success
full dry farming. The settlers who
found the conditions on the dry
lands too hard, did not know how
to combat those conditions. They
did not know what kind of cult
walton to give their growing Oh
dying crops. In brief, they did no
understand the conservation o
moisture or the scientific cult
I walton of soil.
